g.text: MENU
BACK
What is a tesseract?
What style of music is this? What's the right attitude to play it?
Why did you make nested notation? What inspired its creation,
and who inﬂuenced its development?
Is there any kind of admin managing each session?
Is there an end to the piece?
What's the best gear to bring to a nested notation session?
This style of music depends on your perspective. If you aren't playing an instrument or singing,
you might consider it 'multimedia' or 'ﬁxed media'. If you engage with it primarily as a vocalist
or player, since there are no explicit instructions, you might consider it 'modular', 'open',
or even 'free improvisation'. I prefer to think of it as 'nonlinear composition', like a Choose Your
Own Adventure book, or a website.
Two key diﬀerences separate nested notation from Choose Your Own Adventure stories:
recursion
(referencing or repeating itself) and
group choice
. Since a nested notation session
might return more than once to a frame or node, we may deﬁne it as both nonlinear and recursive.
Likewise, since group voting periods are used to move from frame to frame, we may cite
the ability of a group to progress through a session by majority vote as a unique feature of
of nested notation and its type of recursive nonlinearity.
As regards the best attitude for enjoying a nested notaiton session, I recommend seeking an
attitude of
playful acceptance
. The software is not always stable, everyone's devices are
diﬀerent (with diﬀerent volumes), some individuals may be well-expressed while others might
look on wondering what to sing or play.
Should you feel uncomfortable for any reason in a nested notation session, please act with
kindness in self-advocacy. Feel free to speak directly with other participants, change your location
or volume, or even leave the session at any time. It should be fun and challenging! If it is not,
please act for your own good and the good of those around you.
